scenario: Northwind Retailer drives reminder emails in Camunda 8.8 for 4000 carts per day. A modeler needs a time cycle definition that fires every 10 seconds up to five times and a second cycle that fires every day forever with no fixed start time, and the team must encode both as valid ISO 8601 repeating intervals on a timer event.
question: Which pair of cycle expressions matches the documented ISO 8601 repeating-interval format?

options.text: R5/PT10S for every 10 seconds up to five repetitions, and R/P1D for every day infinitely without a fixed start time
PT10S/R5 for every 10 seconds up to five repetitions, and P1D/R for every day infinitely without a fixed start time
5R/PT10S for every 10 seconds up to five repetitions, and R0/P1D for every day infinitely without a fixed start time
R5/T10S for every 10 seconds up to five repetitions, and R/D1 for every day infinitely without a fixed start time

a.text: Correct. The documented format is repetitions then interval. R5/PT10S means every 10 seconds, up to five times. R/P1D omits the repetition count so the timer repeats infinitely until it is cancelled.

b.text: Incorrect. The interval must follow the repetition designator, not precede it, so PT10S/R5 and P1D/R do not match the documented R/duration ordering.

c.text: Incorrect. The repetition count is given as R followed by the number, such as R5, rather than 5R, and R0 would not denote infinite repetitions in the documented format.

d.text: Incorrect. Duration designators require the leading P, and the time component must be prefixed by T, so T10S and D1 are not valid ISO 8601 duration fragments.

explanation: An ISO 8601 repeating interval uses the form R[n]/duration. Examples include R5/PT10S for every 10 seconds up to five times and R/P1D for every day infinitely, where omitting the repetition count means the timer repeats until cancelled.
